facts.title: Speech changes with respect
facts.fact: Korean has several speech levels. The same idea changes form depending on who you address — the verb 먹다 (to eat) becomes polite 먹어요 or honorific 드세요.
facts.korean_en: Please eat slowly. (said warmly to a guest)
facts.note: **존댓말** is polite/honorific speech; **반말** is casual speech used with close friends.

facts.title: Two number systems
facts.fact: Korean uses two sets of numbers: native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋…) for counting things and ages, and Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼…) for dates, money, and minutes.
facts.note: 세 개 uses native 셋 → 세 with the counter **개**. Money uses Sino-Korean: 삼천 원 = 3,000 won.

facts.title: Letters stack into syllable blocks
facts.fact: Hangul letters are not written in a straight line. They are **grouped into square blocks**, one block per syllable, combining a starting consonant, a vowel, and sometimes a final consonant.
facts.korean_en: Hangul is written in grouped blocks.
facts.note: For example **한** packs ㅎ + ㅏ + ㄴ into a single block, read as one syllable.

facts.title: The verb comes last
facts.fact: Korean word order is **subject–object–verb**, so the verb almost always lands at the end of the sentence. You often have to wait until the last word to learn what someone did.
facts.note: Literally the order is "I bread eat." The verb **먹어요** sits at the end.

facts.title: No 'a', 'the', or gender
facts.fact: Korean has **no articles** like "a" or "the" and **no grammatical gender** for nouns. One word like 사과 can mean "apple," "an apple," or "the apple" depending on context.
facts.korean_en: The cat is sleeping. / A cat is sleeping.
facts.note: Listeners rely on **context** to decide whether something is specific or general.

facts.title: Titles 씨 and 님 show respect
facts.fact: Korean attaches respectful titles after names instead of using "Mr." or "Ms." before them. **씨** is polite for peers, and **님** is more formal, used with teachers, customers, and online.
facts.note: Never add **씨** to a full Korean surname alone — it can sound rude. Use it with the given name or full name.

facts.title: Konglish: borrowed English words
facts.fact: Many everyday Korean words are **Konglish** — English loanwords adapted to Korean sounds. Some keep the English meaning, while others have shifted, like 핸드폰 ("hand phone") for a mobile phone.
facts.note: Loanwords are written in **Hangul** and follow Korean sound rules, so 커피 is said "keo-pi," not "coffee."

facts.title: Regional dialects (사투리)
facts.fact: Beyond the Seoul standard, Korea has regional dialects called **사투리**, with different intonation and vocabulary. The Busan and Gyeongsang region is especially known for its strong, distinctive accent.
facts.korean_en: The Busan dialect is really fun.
facts.note: TOPIK uses **표준어** (standard speech), but you may hear 사투리 in dramas and travel.

facts.title: Jeju Island's unique speech
facts.fact: **Jeju** Island has a variety so distinct that many mainland Koreans cannot fully understand it. It preserves old words and sounds, and UNESCO lists traditional Jeju speech as endangered.
facts.korean_en: I want to go to Jeju Island.
facts.note: Some linguists treat **Jeju speech** as a separate language rather than just a dialect.

facts.title: Counting needs counter words
facts.fact: To count things, Korean adds a **counter word** after the number, much like "two *sheets* of paper" in English. Different counters fit different objects: 명 for people, 마리 for animals, 권 for books.
facts.note: Choosing the right counter is key on TOPIK: **두 명** (two people), 책 한 권 (one book), 개 세 마리 (three dogs).

facts.title: Hanja: Chinese characters in Korean
facts.fact: A large part of Korean vocabulary comes from Chinese roots, written historically with **한자 (Hanja)**. Today most text uses only Hangul, but Hanja still appears in names, newspapers, and academic terms.
facts.note: Knowing Hanja roots helps you guess word meanings, since one root like 學 (학, study) appears in 학교, 학생, and 학습.

facts.title: Final consonants (받침)
facts.fact: A consonant at the bottom of a syllable block is called a **받침**. It can change how the next syllable sounds, linking smoothly into the following vowel.
facts.note: For example, **꽃이** is pronounced "꼬치" because the 받침 ㅊ links onto the following vowel.

facts.title: Formal vs. polite sentence endings
facts.fact: Korean sentence endings signal formality. **-습니다/-ㅂ니다** is formal and used in news, presentations, and the military, while **-아요/-어요** is the everyday polite ending.
facts.note: On TOPIK writing, the formal **-습니다** style is often expected; spoken practice usually uses **-어요**.

facts.title: Topic 은/는 vs. subject 이/가
facts.fact: Korean marks nouns with particles. **은/는** sets the topic or contrast, while **이/가** marks the grammatical subject. Choosing between them shifts the nuance of a sentence.
facts.korean_en: As for me, I'm a student.
facts.note: Use **는** after a vowel (저는) and **은** after a consonant (책은); likewise 가 after a vowel and 이 after a consonant.

facts.title: Marking the object with 을/를
facts.fact: The thing receiving an action takes the **object particle 을/를**. It tells the listener which noun the verb acts on, which matters because Korean word order is flexible.
facts.note: Use **을** after a consonant (물을) and **를** after a vowel (사과를). In casual speech the particle is often dropped.

facts.title: One greeting for any time of day
facts.fact: Korean does not split greetings into morning, afternoon, and evening. **안녕하세요** works at any hour, and it literally asks whether you are at peace.
facts.note: Casual friends shorten it to **안녕**, which also means "goodbye."

facts.title: The honorific verb infix -(으)시-
facts.fact: To respect the *person doing the action*, Korean inserts **-(으)시-** into the verb. So 가다 (to go) becomes 가세요 when the subject is someone you honor, like a grandparent.
facts.note: Some verbs have special honorific forms instead of just adding **-시-**: 자다 → 주무시다, 먹다 → 드시다.

facts.title: Spelling and sound can differ
facts.fact: Korean has regular **sound-change rules**, so a word is not always pronounced the way it is spelled. A final ㄱ before ㄴ, for example, softens into an ㅇ sound.
facts.note: **국물** is read "궁물" because ㄱ assimilates to the following ㅁ — a change called nasalization.
